title: Oppression & Mismanagement
content:
Session 9: Interplay with company law 
Needle Industries (India) Ltd v Needle Industries Newey (India) Holding Ltd [1981 | SC | 3 J] 
Facts: 
207 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã NIIL was an Indian company whose majority shareholding was held by a holding company incorporated in the UK, which 
in turn was owned by two UK companies, Needle Industries Studley Ltd and Newey (India) Ltd ‚áí as a result, non-resident 
shareholding in NIIL exceeded 60%. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Pursuant to directives issued by the RBI under the Foreign Exchange Regulation Act, 1973 (FERA), NIIL was required to 
reduce non-resident equity participation to 40% within a stipulated period & failure to do so would render continuation of 
its business illegal under section 29 of FERA. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The MD and Indian directors proposed that the excess shareholding be diluted through a rights issue offered to existing 
shareholders BUT the foreign holding company could not lawfully subscribe to further shares, as acceptance would violate 
FERA and RBI directions. 
‚û¢‚Äã A rights issue of 16,000 equity shares at par was approved and allotted exclusively to Indian shareholders.  
‚û¢‚Äã Due to delay in postal communication to the UK, the holding company did not receive the offer in time and was 
effectively excluded from participation ‚Üí resulted in dilution of the foreign holding company‚Äôs majority stake, 
converting it into a minority. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The holding company filed a petition under sections 397 and 398 of the Companies Act alleging oppression, 
mismanagement, mala fide conduct, breach of fiduciary duties, and violation of the proper purpose rule. 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the issue and allotment of rights shares exclusively to Indian shareholders amounted to oppression or 
mismanagement. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the directors had acted mala fide or for an improper purpose by diluting the majority shareholder. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether compliance with FERA and RBI directives justified actions that incidentally prejudiced the majority shareholder. 
 
Rule: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Under company law, directors owe fiduciary duties to act bona fide in the interests of the company and must exercise powers 
such as share issuance for a proper purpose, not merely to alter voting power. 
‚ùñ‚Äã s29 of FERA ‚Üí continuation of business by an Indian company with excess non-resident shareholding without RBI 
permission is illegal. Acceptance of shares by a non-resident in breach of RBI directives constitutes a statutory violation. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Conduct is ‚Äúoppressive‚Äù only if it is burdensome, harsh and wrongful, and not merely because it disadvantages a 
shareholder. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã REJECTED the claim of oppression and mismanagement. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The dominant purpose of the rights issue was not to disenfranchise the foreign holding company, but to comply with 
mandatory statutory requirements under FERA.  
‚û¢‚Äã Continuation of NIIL‚Äôs business beyond the stipulated date without dilution of foreign equity would have been 
illegal ‚Üí directors therefore faced a legal compulsion: either the holding company had to disinvest voluntarily, or 
the company had to issue further shares to Indian shareholders. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The foreign holding company had no legal right to insist on participating in the rights issue, because acceptance of the shares 
would itself have violated FERA and RBI directions ‚áí a shareholder cannot complain of exclusion from an option which it 
was legally disabled from exercising. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The fact that the rights issue incidentally resulted in dilution of the foreign majority was held to be a consequence, not the 
motive, of the directors‚Äô action ‚áí the conversion of the majority into a minority was not an abuse of power but the 
inevitable outcome of lawful compliance. 
‚ùñ‚Äã On fiduciary duties, directors are under a higher duty to obey the law than to preserve any particular shareholder‚Äôs 
dominance.  
‚û¢‚Äã When statutory compliance requires action adverse to certain shareholders, directors are not acting mala fide 
merely because such shareholders are prejudiced. 
 
208 
 
Conclusion: The issue and allotment of rights shares to Indian shareholders was neither oppressive nor an act of mismanagement, 
but a bona fide exercise of powers undertaken to comply with FERA and RBI directives ‚Üí the directors acted for a proper purpose 
and in the interests of the company. The petition under sections 397 and 398 was dismissed. 
 
LIC of India v Escorts Ltd [1985 | SC | 5 J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The dispute arose under FERA and the Non-Resident External Account Rules, 1970, which permitted portfolio investment 
by non-resident entities subject to the condition that at least 60% control or beneficial interest vested in non-resident 
Indians.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Thirteen England-incorporated Caparo companies, owned by the Caparo Group, invested in Escorts group companies, 
claiming eligibility on the basis that 61.6% of their shares were held by the Swaraj Paul Family Trust, whose beneficiaries 
were non-resident Indians.  
‚û¢‚Äã On this basis, RBI granted permission under section 29 of FERA to acquire shares in Escorts Ltd. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Escorts Ltd nevertheless refused to register the transfers, alleging suppression of material facts, violation of the purpose of 
FERA, and undue pressure by LIC and other financial institutions.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Escorts consequently filed a writ petition challenging the legality of the share acquisitions and the role of state-controlled 
institutions in facilitating them. 
 
Issues:  
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether, in cases where the transfer of shares to non-residents is regulated by statute, a company has the discretion to refuse 
registration even after permission has been granted by the Reserve Bank of India. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the absence of prior permission under section 29 of FERA renders the transfer of shares void or merely 
unenforceable until permission is obtained. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether a vendor-purchaser constructive trust arises in favour of a non-resident transferee in the absence of RBI 
permission. 
‚ùñ‚Äã To what extent, if any, courts or companies may lift the corporate veil to examine compliance with FERA and the Portfolio 
Investment Scheme. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Where share transfers are governed by statute, specifically transfers to non-residents under FERA, statutory permission is 
mandatory ‚Üí in the absence of RBI approval, a transferee acquires no enforceable right to be entered on the register of 
members.  
‚û¢‚Äã Equitable doctrines such as the vendor‚Äìpurchaser constructive trust do not apply where statutory permission is a 
condition precedent to a valid transfer, and no beneficial interest arises without such approval ‚áí Escorts Ltd was 
justified in refusing registration until RBI permission was obtained.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Once permission is granted, however, the company has no discretion to refuse registration or to question the merits of the 
RBI‚Äôs decision, as exclusive authority under FERA vests in the RBI. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Since s.29(1) of FERA does not require ‚Äúprevious‚Äù permission, the RBI may grant approval ex post facto.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Transactions without prior permission are not void ab initio, but only unenforceable until permission is obtained.  
‚ùñ‚Äã On corporate veil lifting ‚Üí narrow approach adopted ‚áí permitting it only to the extent expressly contemplated by FERA 
and the Portfolio Investment Scheme, namely to ascertain whether the required level of NRI beneficial ownership existed ‚Üí 
broader veil-lifting was impermissible. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Finally, while companies and courts cannot ordinarily question RBI approvals, such permission may be challenged under 
Article 226 on limited grounds such as mala fides, non-application of mind, or action contrary to national interest. 
 
Conclusion: Escorts Ltd was not entitled to refuse registration of the transfer of shares once permission had been granted by the RBI 
under section 29 of FERA. Regulatory approval by the RBI is determinative and binding, and companies cannot challenge or 
second-guess such approval. Share transfers made without prior permission are not void ab initio and may be validated through 
209 
 
subsequent or conditional permission. Lifting of the corporate veil under FERA is permissible only to the limited extent necessary to 
ascertain compliance with statutory requirements and no further. 
 
Dale & Carrington Investment (P) Ltd v P.K. Prathapan [2004 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The dispute concerns a closely held private company formed to run a hotel. The respondents, P.K. Prathapan and his wife, 
were shareholders, while the appellant, Ramanujam, was a director, shareholder, and in effective control of the company‚Äôs 
management. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Prathapan claimed that he provided financial assistance to the company by remitting money to Ramanujam, with the 
understanding that shares would be issued to him in return. Ramanujam disputed this, contending that the remittances 
were personal loans arising out of their close relationship. It was undisputed that the funds were transferred to Ramanujam 
personally and not to the company. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Subsequently, the authorised share capital was increased, and in a board meeting chaired by Ramanujam, additional shares 
were allotted to him. Prathapan alleged that he was not informed of either the increase in share capital or the allotment, and 
that these steps were taken to dilute his shareholding and entrench Ramanujam‚Äôs control. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Prathapan filed a petition under Sections 397 and 398 of the Companies Act, 1956, alleging oppression, mismanagement, 
and breach of fiduciary duty. The Company Law Board held that Ramanujam‚Äôs conduct was oppressive and granted 
Prathapan the option to sell his shares at a fair value. On appeal, the High Court set aside the allotment of shares to 
Ramanujam, holding it to be illegal and oppressive. 
 
Issue: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the further issue and allotment of shares in favour of Ramanujam violated the proper purpose rule and constituted 
oppression under Sections 397 and 398 of the Companies Act. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether Prathapan and his wife lacked locus standi to maintain the oppression and mismanagement petition on account of 
not having obtained prior permission from the RBI under the FERA in respect of transfer or holding of shares in their 
favour. 
 
Rule: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Although directors possess the power to issue further share capital, such power must be exercised bona fide, for a proper 
corporate purpose, and in the interest of the company as a whole.  
‚û¢‚Äã An issue of shares undertaken primarily to dilute the shareholding of an existing shareholder or to perpetuate 
control in the hands of the management amounts to a breach of fiduciary duty and constitutes oppression. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Directors stand in a fiduciary position vis-√Ý-vis the company and its shareholders.  
‚û¢‚Äã Any exercise of power which is tainted by mala fides, lack of transparency, or exclusion of a shareholder from 
participation in corporate decision-making violates the principles underlying Sections 397 and 398. 
‚ùñ‚Äã FERA / FEMA ‚Äì Permission and Locus Standi 
‚û¢‚Äã Permission of the RBI under FERA for holding or transfer of shares could be obtained ex post facto.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The absence of prior permission does not render the shareholding void ab initio. 
‚û¢‚Äã FERA had since been repealed and replaced by the FEMA, which does not contain provisions imposing similar 
disabilities affecting the validity of shareholding or locus standi in company law proceedings. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The further issue of shares in favour of Ramanujam was not made for a proper purpose.  
‚û¢‚Äã The issue was neither motivated by genuine capital requirements of the company nor undertaken in a transparent 
manner.  
‚û¢‚Äã Instead, it was designed to increase Ramanujam‚Äôs control and reduce Prathapan‚Äôs stake without offering him a 
proportionate opportunity to subscribe to the new shares. 
210 
 
‚ùñ‚Äã The conduct of Ramanujam, who chaired the board meeting approving the allotment in his own favour without proper 
disclosure to Prathapan, amounted to a breach of fiduciary duty. The allotment therefore constituted oppression within the 
meaning of Sections 397 and 398. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Even assuming RBI permission was required, the same could be obtained ex post facto. Following LIC v Escorts, the absence 
of such permission did not invalidate Prathapan‚Äôs shareholding or disentitle him from invoking statutory remedies under 
the Companies Act. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Since FERA had been repealed and FEMA did not impose comparable restrictions, the argument based on foreign exchange 
regulation was in any event devoid of substance. 
 
Conclusion: The further issue and allotment of shares in favour of Ramanujam violated the proper purpose rule and constituted 
oppressive conduct. Prathapan and his wife had the requisite locus standi to file the petition under Sections 397 and 398, 
notwithstanding the absence of prior RBI permission under FERA. The allotment of shares in favour of Ramanujam was rightly set 
aside. 
 
 
211
